title: The Nativity of Our Lord: Christmas Eve
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 25, 2022 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Christmas Day itself, the Feast of the Nativity of Our Lord, the most significant celebration in the Christian calendar after Easter. The lectionary readings for Christmas Day (RCL) would have included Isaiah 52:7-10, Psalm 98, Hebrews 1:1-4 (5-12), and John 1:1-14, focusing on the incarnation and the Word made flesh. The Church calendar places this at the culmination of Advent, moving into the Christmas season that extends through Epiphany (January 6, 2023). Sung Eucharist services at an Episcopal cathedral would have been elaborate, with full choir and festive liturgical elements reflecting the solemnity of the feast.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 80th General Convention (held July-August 2022 in Baltimore), which had confirmed the church's progressive trajectory on LGBTQ+ inclusion and other social issues. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (who began her tenure in 2021), was navigating post-pandemic rebuilding and reconciliation efforts. No major episcopal succession or controversy was active in the Rocky Mountain region at this specific moment, allowing for a relatively stable liturgical calendar. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the seat of the Diocese of Colorado, would have been hosting the bishop for Christmas celebrations and serving as the liturgical center of diocesan life.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing unseasonably mild weather for late December 2022, with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and minimal snow cover—unusual for Christmas in Colorado and a topic of local conversation. The city was preparing for New Year's celebrations and post-holiday shopping season, with downtown Denver's Capitol Hill district featuring holiday decorations and events. No major crises or acute local news events dominated the week immediately preceding December 25, allowing the Christmas season to proceed with relative normalcy following two years of pandemic-constrained celebrations. The Colorado Springs area had experienced a devastating nightclub shooting at Club Q on November 19, 2022 (five weeks prior), which would have cast a shadow of grief and vigil across Colorado faith communities, including reflections likely touching Advent preaching in the weeks leading up to Christmas.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Biden administration and Congress were navigating the FTX cryptocurrency exchange collapse and Sam Bankman-Fried's arrest (November 2022), which dominated financial and political news with implications for campaign finance and regulation. The midterm election aftermath was still being processed, with Republicans having gained House control but Democrats retaining Senate control in the November 8 elections, defying historical precedent. The nation's media landscape was consumed with Elon Musk's chaotic acquisition of Twitter (completed October 2022) and the subsequent mass layoffs and platform turbulence. The COVID-19 pandemic, while no longer dominating headlines, remained a background concern as new variants circulated and vaccination debates continued, though Christmas 2022 represented the first largely unrestricted holiday season since 2019.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's invasion of Ukraine (February 2022) was entering its tenth month with winter warfare accelerating; the humanitarian crisis was deepening as Russian forces targeted energy infrastructure, leaving millions without heat and electricity heading into winter. China's zero-COVID protests (sparked by the November 25 Urumqi apartment fire) had evolved into the broadest public dissent in years, with demonstrations in major cities and calls for Xi Jinping to step down, forcing the government to begin rolling back strict lockdown policies. The Iranian government was intensifying its crackdown on the Mahsa Amini protests, which had erupted in September 2022 following her death in police custody, with executions of protesters becoming a focal point of international condemnation and human rights concern.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The United States was in a complex cultural moment: exhaustion from pandemic disruption and political polarization was mixing with a desire to return to "normal" holiday traditions, creating a somewhat nostalgic and intensely commercialized Christmas season. The FIFA World Cup in Qatar (November 28-December 18, 2022) had just concluded with Argentina's Lionel Messi finally winning the trophy, a cultural moment that eclipsed much American sports attention just days before Christmas. A pervasive sense of economic anxiety characterized the zeitgeist, with inflation at 7.1% (down from 9.1% in June but still historically high), recession fears circulating, and ordinary families grappling with higher costs for heating, food, and holiday celebrations. Entertainment and streaming dominated leisure time; The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power and House of the Dragon had captured cultural attention, while traditional Christmas media (It's a Wonderful Life, holiday specials) provided counterbalance to the year's turbulence. Denver's cultural life, as a growing metropolitan area, was navigating the tension between rapid development, rising costs of living, and a desire for community cohesion—themes that would resonate implicitly in a Christmas sermon about incarnational presence and belonging.
